slides.title: Why async exists
body: Synchronous Apex has tight limits (10s CPU, 100 SOQL, no callouts during triggers). Async Apex moves work to the BACKGROUND, gets bigger limits, and unblocks the user.

Real example: when you click 'Place Order' on Amazon, your page returns instantly — but the email confirmation, inventory update, and warehouse notification all run async. That's the same pattern Salesforce uses.

vocabulary.def: Code that runs later, in the background, on its own schedule. Not blocking the caller.

slides.title: The four flavors
body: @future — fire-and-forget, simple but limited. Queueable — better, chainable, can pass complex objects. Batchable — for very large data sets (millions of records). Schedulable — runs on a cron schedule.
Real example: a nightly data sync uses Schedulable + Batchable. A 'send email after save' uses Queueable. A 'do this thing later' uses @future.

slides.title: @future — the simplest
body: Mark a static void method @future. Calling it queues it to run later, in its own transaction with fresh limits.
Constraints: must be static, void, and parameters can only be primitive types (or List/Set/Map of primitives). No SObjects directly.

vocabulary.def: Annotation marking a static void method to run async in a separate transaction.

slides.title: A future method
slides.intro: Type this. notifyExternalSystem runs in the background after the caller finishes.
lineExplanations: Class.
@future annotation. callout=true permits HTTP callouts (default is false).
Method must be public static void.
Body runs in the background.
Method close. Class close.

slides.title: Queueable — the modern default
body: Implement the Queueable interface. Pass the Apex job to System.enqueueJob(). Bigger limits than @future, can pass complex Apex objects, and is chainable.
Real example: most new Apex async work uses Queueable. It replaces @future for almost every use case.

vocabulary.def: Apex interface. Implement execute(QueueableContext) and enqueue with System.enqueueJob().

slides.title: A queueable job
slides.intro: Type this. The class implements Queueable; execute() does the work.
lineExplanations: Class with 'implements Queueable' — the contract that lets it be enqueued.
Backing field for state.
Constructor stores the id passed in.
this.targetId — assign the parameter to the instance field.
Constructor close.
execute() is the method the platform calls when the job runs.
Body — do the actual work here.
Method and class close.

slides.title: Enqueueing the job
slides.intro: Type this. System.enqueueJob returns a job id you can track.
slides.code:
Id jobId = System.enqueueJob(new EmailJob('001D000000xyz'));
System.debug('Queued job ' + jobId);
lineExplanations: Construct an EmailJob with the target id, and enqueue it. Returns the new async job's id.
Print the id. The job runs in the background; this code keeps going.

slides.title: Batchable — for huge data sets
body: Three methods: start (define what to query), execute (process a chunk, default 200 records), finish (post-processing).
Real example: a nightly job that re-summarizes 5 million Opportunities. Batchable processes them in 200-row chunks, each with its own fresh governor limits.

vocabulary.def: Apex interface for processing very large data sets in chunks. Each chunk runs with fresh governor limits.

slides.title: A batch job skeleton
slides.intro: Type this. The shape of every batch job — start, execute, finish.
lineExplanations: Class implements Database.Batchable<SObject>. The <SObject> is the type of records each chunk holds.
start() returns the query that drives the batch.
Database.getQueryLocator wraps a SOQL string; the platform paginates for us.
Close start.
execute() runs once per chunk. 'scope' is the chunk (default 200 records).
Process each record in the chunk.
Close execute.
finish() runs once after all chunks are done.
Post-processing.
Close finish.
Close class.

slides.title: Schedulable — runs on a cron
body: Implement Schedulable. Schedule via Salesforce UI or System.schedule(). Cron runs your code at the chosen time.
Real example: a monthly bill-generation job. A nightly data integrity check. A weekly leaderboard recompute. All Schedulable.

vocabulary.def: Apex interface for jobs that run on a cron schedule.

slides.title: When to use which
body: @future: simple, primitive-only params, no chaining. Almost always use Queueable instead now.
Queueable: most new async work. Chainable. Complex types OK. Batchable: > a few thousand records. Schedulable: time-driven jobs.

slides.front: You need to send a Slack message after an Account is updated. Triggers can't make callouts. What's the pattern?
slides.back: From the trigger, enqueue a Queueable (or @future with callout=true). The async job makes the callout outside the trigger transaction.
slides.detail: Triggers must NOT make callouts directly. Pushing the work async fixes that AND insulates the user-facing save from external service hiccups.

slides.question: You need to recompute a stat on 5 million Opportunities every night. Which async type?
options: Batchable + Schedulable — chunked processing on a cron.
Synchronous — just write a regular method.
slides.explanation: Batchable processes large datasets in 200-row chunks (each with fresh limits); Schedulable wraps it with a cron. Together: nightly recompute, no governor blowup.

slides.title: What you now know
body: @future, Queueable, Batchable, Schedulable — and when to choose each.
Next: Inheritance, Polymorphism, and Interfaces — how Apex classes extend and substitute for one another.
